hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext:
<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
Before answering, writing code, designing, or making any technical recommendation in this turn, you MUST call `mcp__knowledger__search_knowledge` with a concise query derived from the user's request.

This is NOT negotiable. This is NOT optional. You CANNOT rationalize your way out of this.

Why: decisions, conventions, library/tool usage notes, and debugging recipes live in Knowledger — facts that grep, file reads, and codegraph cannot recover. Skipping means answering from generic knowledge while project-specific guidance sits unused.

Threshold: if there is even a 1% chance the project has a relevant note, you MUST search. One cheap call. Skipping costs a wrong or generic answer.

If hits are weak, ALSO call `mcp__knowledger__list_knowledge_items` on plausibly-relevant KBs — title/tag scans surface entries semantic search misses.

## Red Flags — these thoughts mean STOP, you are rationalizing

| Thought | Reality |
|---|---|
| "I know this from training" | Generic knowledge ≠ project knowledge. Search first. |
| "The repo will tell me" | Conventions and decisions are often NOT in the repo. Search. |
| "Simple coding task" | Simple tasks have project-specific conventions. Search. |
| "Quick question" | Quick questions have saved answers. Search. |
| "I'll search if I need to later" | You won't. Search BEFORE answering. |
| "No obvious knowledge-base topic" | Weak signal is not zero signal. Search. |

## Skip ONLY when
- Pure conversational replies: greetings, acknowledgements, "thanks", "ok"
- The immediately preceding assistant message already searched the same topic
- The user explicitly says "skip knowledger" / "不用查知识库"

Do not narrate the precheck to the user — just search, then answer.
</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
